quotes.verbatim_quote:
To understand the function of a chord, you must look at both the
chord that comes before it and after it.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the chapter's central harmonic principle, establishing that chord analysis is relational rather than absolute.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A secondary dominant is when a dominant 7 chord acts as a V chord
of a diatonic chord other than the tonic. We call this “tonicization.” This
means the chord the secondary dominant precedes now sounds like a
new tonic to the listener.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines secondary dominants and tonicization as new vocabulary essential to understanding the harmonic analysis of this and future chapters.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Formula for a major 7 chord: Root-3"-5"-7%
Formula for a dominant 7 chord: Root-3"-5'-b7
Formula for a minor 7 chord: Root-b3-5"-b7
Formula for a half-diminished chord: Root-b3-b5-b7
Formula for a diminished 7 chord: Root-b3-b5-bb7
quotes.why_load_bearing: Provides the explicit interval formulas that underpin all chord tone identification and improvisation work throughout the book.

quotes.topic: guide tones as primary chord tones
quotes.verbatim_quote:
The good news is we already have identified the most important chord
tones, which are the guide tones. Continue to let the 3rds and 7ths of
each chord be your guide. But let’s start utilizing these other ones as
well, because ultimately over and above scales we want to be impro-
vising off of these.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the pedagogical hierarchy that chord tones—not scales—are the primary material for improvisation, bridging the guide tone concept from earlier chapters.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In some cases, you will see a “CA” over a note. This stands for “chro-
matic approach,” meaning the note in question does not represent a
basic chord tone, extension or alteration, but is simply chromatically
approaching a diatonic note. In some cases, they are connecting two
diatonic notes together.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the chromatic approach as a named technique, distinguishing it from structural chord tones and providing a framework for understanding non-chord-tone melodic movement.

quotes.topic: chord tone arpeggio voice leading rule
quotes.verbatim_quote:
The chord tones are played as arpeggios, meaning they always move
in order. However, the first chord tone of a given chord may not always
be the root. | have written this exercise using voice leading as a rule of
thumb. Whatever the last note played in a particular bar was, | move
to the nearest chord tone of the next chord. You'll notice that often
this note is even the same note as the previous bar.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the voice-leading rule governing the chord tone exercise, teaching smooth arpeggio connection across chord changes as a concrete technique.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Regardless of which chord tone is started on, they always go in order.
Root-3-5%-7*, or 3rd-5%-7%-Root, or 5%-7%-Root-3"...etc.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the invariant ordering rule for arpeggios regardless of starting note, which is the structural constraint that makes the chord tone exercise work as a mapping tool.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Don’t get too hung up on the iv minor chord. In your improvisation, you
will simply want to consider the difference in the chord quality. Don’t
be playing major thirds or major 7ths!
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives a concrete negative rule for improvising over the iv minor chord, warning against the most common quality-mismatch error.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In bar 25 we start with the IV chord. It transitions to a iv minor chord,
which you could analyze as a jump into the key of C minor. Note that
| do list some alternative chord changes above what | would consider
the “basic” ones. Essentially you can start a cycle of fourths from the
iv minor.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explains the IV-to-iv minor pivot as a key-of-C-minor tonicization and introduces the cycle-of-fourths as an analytical lens for the substitution chords.
